issue: Fulfillment of Article VI disarmament obligations

entries.position_summary: The country welcomes broad support for risk reduction measures and asserts that such measures complement Article VI disarmament objectives, contributing to the prevention of nuclear war. It frames risk reduction as aligned with the NPT’s disarmament commitments.
entries.quote: Second, we would like to welcome the progress made on key issues. For example, we welcome the strong support of many States Parties for risk reduction. As we have frequently stated, risk reduction complements Article VI goals and contributes to our common interest in preventing nuclear war.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its Article VI obligation to pursue good-faith negotiations on nuclear disarmament and commits to doing so through bilateral treaties, multilateral fora, and practical actions to advance NPT disarmament goals.
entries.quote: The United States stands by our obligation under the NPT to pursue negotiations in good faith on effective measures on nuclear disarmament -- in bilateral treaties, through multilateral fora, and actions to advance NPT disarmament goals.

entries.position_summary: The country supports negotiating an FMCT and urges China to join other NPT nuclear-weapon states in adopting a moratorium on producing fissile material for nuclear weapons pending such a treaty, framing this as part of fulfilling Article VI obligations.
entries.quote: Article VI. Pending such a treaty, we hope the PRC will join the other NPT nuclear- weapon States in adopting a moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights it has declassified information on its nuclear stockpile and argues that greater transparency of nuclear arsenals supports nonproliferation and disarmament, including fulfilling NPT commitments. It frames transparency as a concrete step toward meeting treaty obligations.
entries.quote: And it is why the United States just released newly declassified information on our nuclear weapons stockpile. We believe that increasing the transparency of states’ nuclear stockpiles is important to nonproliferation and disarmament efforts, including commitments under this treaty.

entries.position_summary: Supports regular reporting by States parties on implementing the 2010 Action Plan and Article VI, and encourages nuclear-weapon States to adopt standardized reporting formats and agreed intervals within the strengthened review process.
entries.quote: 7. States parties have long reported to the Review Conference on their actions in support of the Non-Proliferation Treaty. As was stated in the consensus action plan from the 2010 Review Conference, “States parties should submit regular reports, within the framework of the strengthened review process for the Treaty, on the implementation of the present action plan, as well as of article VI”, along with disarmament actions recommended by the 1995 and 2000 Review Conferences (action 20). The action plan also encouraged the nuclear-weapon States to agree on a standard reporting form and to determine appropriate reporting intervals (action 21).

entries.position_summary: The United States supports negotiating an FMCT, has engaged in UNGA FMCT initiatives, and maintains a unilateral moratorium on producing fissile material for weapons while urging China to follow suit. It cites irreversible disarmament steps—dismantling plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions via arms control with Russia, and fissile material disposition—and endorses the principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. The U.S. advances verification capabilities through the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE.
entries.quote: We will also engage constructively in the other subsidiary bodies, including on the Cessation of the nuclear arms race and nuclear disarmament. In addition to our efforts at the CD, the United States actively participated in two UN General Assembly FMCT initiatives in 2014-2015 and 2017-2018 that we believe laid important groundwork for future FMCT negotiations. We continue to maintain our unilateral moratorium on the production of fissile material for use in nuclear weapons and urge the PRC – the only Nuclear Weapon State that has not done the same – to join us. 2000 Steps Five and Thirteen; 2010 Actions Two and Eighteen: Irreversibility and Further Development of Verification Capabilities: On Steps Five and Thirteen, the United States has undertaken important steps to support the principal of irreversibility, including through irreversible dismantlement of all weapons-grade plutonium production reactors, stockpile reductions through arms control treaties with Russia, disposition of fissile material from nuclear weapons, and other efforts. We also support language from the 2022 RevCon Draft Final document that acknowledges the mutually reinforcing principles of irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency. Active U.S. engagement in the IPNDV, Quad Nuclear Verification Partnership, and UN GGE underscore our commitment to engaging a broad set of countries on the development of future tools for arms control that support irreversibility, verifiability, and transparency in nuclear disarmament. We also welcome the group statement on irreversibility in nuclear disarmament delivered by Norway,

entries.position_summary: The United States argues against adopting benchmarks, targets, and timelines for nuclear disarmament within the NPT review process, asserting they overlook worsening security conditions, national security needs, practical and political constraints, and risks of non-compliance. It views such proposals as not improving the review process.
entries.quote: However, the United States also recognizes there are some proposals that would not improve the review process. For instance, proposals that seek to establish benchmarks, targets, and timelines for nuclear disarmament may appear beneficial, but fail to recognize the worsening security environment, national security concerns, practical challenges, political realities, and the risk of non-compliance.

entries.position_summary: The United States underscores regular national reporting to NPT meetings, including its Nuclear Posture Review, as evidence of a strong commitment to transparency and progress on disarmament. It supports enhancing reporting and interactive discussion to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process and will incorporate state feedback to expand future reports.
entries.quote: The United States submitted national reports to the 2014 NPT Preparatory Committee, an update for the 2015 NPT Review Conference, an update including on actions over the previous six years for the 2022 Review Conference, and an update to that report providing a link to our 2022 Nuclear Posture Review. Before the practice of “national reports,” the United States submitted similar information to previous Review Conferences. Collectively, these demonstrate the steady U.S. commitment to transparency and making real progress on disarmament. We welcome constructive efforts to improve NPT reporting and strongly support calls to improve transparency and accountability in the NPT review process through interactive discussion of national reports. As we approach future U.S. reports, we will consider feedback from interested States regarding specific transparency elements they would find helpful. Based on that feedback, we will seek to address additional topics in our future national reports where possible.

entries.position_summary: The United States reaffirms its commitment to disarmament consistent with the Thirteen Steps and Article VI, stating readiness to pursue critical arms control measures. It seeks to reduce nuclear risks through bilateral engagement with Russia and China and via the P5 process, despite their current unwillingness to engage.
entries.quote: The “Thirteen Steps” clearly identified the need for all Nuclear Weapon States to engage in the disarmament process. It is because of the seriousness with which we take our disarmament-related commitments that President Biden made clear at the UN last September that “no matter what else is happening in the world, the United States is ready to pursue critical arms control measures.” It's also why we will continue to seek to reduce nuclear risks with Russia and the PRC bilaterally and through the P5 process, despite their unwillingness to engage.

entries.position_summary: The United States emphasizes transparency through its NPR and stockpile disclosures, reports steep unilateral reductions including in non-strategic weapons, and highlights measures lowering operational status and the role of nuclear weapons in its strategy. It encourages China and Russia to adopt similar transparency. This is presented as part of fulfilling disarmament commitments and advancing risk reduction.
entries.quote: well as in the public release of information in our Nuclear Posture Review and pertaining to our nuclear weapons stockpile. The U.S. national report to the 2022 RevCon also provided detailed information on other relevant topics, including steep U.S. reductions in non-strategic nuclear weapons and other unilateral cuts beyond treaty-based reductions over the past three decades, significant measures taken to reduce operational status of nuclear weapons systems, and the reduced role of nuclear weapons in U.S. national security strategy, including with respect to the 2022 NPR’s Declaratory Policy. We encourage the PRC and Russia to provide similar transparency.
